records.ety: [From F. dfait, OF. desfait, p. p. ofe dfaire, OF. desfaire, to undo; L. dis- + facere to do. See Feat, Fact, and cf. Disfashion.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Defeated; p. pr. & vb. n. Defeating.]

defs: To undo; to disfigure; to destroy.
quotes: His unkindness may defeat my life.

defs: To render null and void, as a title; to frustrate, as hope; to deprive, as of an estate.
quotes: He finds himself naturally to dread a superior Being that can defeat all his designs, and disappoint all his hopes.
The escheators . . . defeated the right heir of his succession.
In one instance he defeated his own purpose.

defs: To overcome or vanquish, as an army; to check, disperse, or ruin by victory; to overthrow.
To resist with success; as, to defeat an assault.
quotes: Sharp reasons to defeat the law.

records.ety: [Cf. F. dfaite, fr. dfaire. See Defeat, v.]

quotes: Upon whose property and most dear life A damned defeat was made.

defs: Frustration by rendering null and void, or by prevention of success; as, the defeat of a plan or design.
An overthrow, as of an army in battle; loss of a battle; repulse suffered; discomfiture; -- opposed to victory.
